Dear Sir Jos^h

It is with much pleasure that I send you the
last Number of English Fungi which is so compleated that you
although as it were the father of natural science in regard to
a vast proportion generally in Europe I may say you expected to find
that I sometimes forget the lapse of time I shall not forget to take
that affectionate leave of you in a constant intercourse with
any other works as they are published -

For your keeping an open library so publickly beneficial
and encouraging the sciences is owing in a great measure the
owing that this work the 1st in the whole world has been
so compleated for likewise Flora Danica &c though
too large & too highly finished to be taken old of
feel very short happy is the man of my esteem & you
I'm also for many tokens of your friendship shewn me
to me in the space of three & twenty years past

wishing yourself a grateful share in
enjoying & happy long & independent myself in
Days

my faithful
Humble Servt
Jas Sowerby

[written vertically on the right edge]
To Great Britain

Honourable Gentlemen

I beg leave to forward my [inserted above: fervent] grateful thanks to
your Honourable Board for your award determined to me
on the 5^th inst. I cannot fully express myself, yet you
may be assured that the thanks of the Lords Commissioners of
the Admiralty added to your honourable confirmation of
my having been at all useful in so great a concern to my
nation as the British Navy - It binds me with the most
ardent desire to do my duty and I shall be happy at
all times to meet the approbation of a Tribunal of so
much experience as your Honourable Board. May you
enjoy a continuance of happy years and the British Navy
flourish under your arduous endeavours

I am
Hon: Gent:^n
Your very faithful
humble Servant
Jas Sowerby

[circled: Copy]

11^th Oct. 1814

Honourable Gentlemen

I have the pleasure of sending the
sixteen Hygrometers coloured. It would be wrong not
to say a word or two about them and to observe that
in all probability they would spoil where wood is liable
to mischief. I therefore recommend that when not in
immediate use they should be preserved in a moderate
temperature and in [text crossed out: such] a situation where the red
indicates a safe medium. And I would intimate
that it is rather safer to depend upon yellow as [inserted above: too] dry,
and blue as generally too damp for the preservation
of wood above water as rather more accurate than
[text crossed out: on] the whole scale of the instrument, allowing from
the red on either side the degrees of dry & wet as more
dangerous as the hand is [inserted above: distant] in degrees from it. Thus if
getting too dry, assist it with shade &c & if too wet
with a proportionate medium of air.

I am Honourable Gentlemen, Your very obed^t Serv^t
Jas Sowerby

Navy Office 3^d. March 1814.-

Sir,

Commissioner Cunningham having
transmitted to us a letter from the Master
Shipwright of Deptford Yard, stating that you
have expressed a wish that the space
chosen by you for stowing Timber conformably
to your views, should be paved with Purbeck
Stone, and represented that in conversation
with you, he proposed [inserted above: that] the space in question
should be paved with common Stone, to which
you objected; - We request you will let us know
what are your objections to the Ground being
paved with common Stone strewed over with
Smith's Cinders.-

We are,
Sir,
Your humble Servants,
T.B. Thompson
F.T. Hartwell
H Legge

James Sowerby Esq^r

Navy Office.

. Navy Office 5.^th January 1814.

Sir,

Having had under consideration your letter of the
Ultimo, We acquaint you that we have awarded to
you the sum of Two hundred Guineas for the
services which you have rendered in order to
prevent as much as possible the alarming
increase of the Dry Rot in His Majestys
Ships, and to introduce an improved
mode of seasoning and stowing away
Timber. - At the same time we deem
it necessary to apprize you that in
awarding this sum we do not attach
to you the merit of having saved
the Queen Charlotte by your advice,
all the defective Materials having

James Sowerby Esq^r.
been

been taken out of that Ship at
Plymouth, when she was repaired
there..

We are,
Sir,
Your humble Servants,
T.B. Thompson
R. Seppings
H Legge

Rec^d the money at the bank Jan 15^th 1813 -
J S

1817
Sept^r 15^th went to the Navy office at M^r Seppings request who took me
up the private stairs to one of the model rooms to ask my
oppinion, privately, upon, regarding some of his difficulties
and to instruct him in my oppinion regarding forceing a
certain proportion of salt into certain timbers in a
ships hold &c. Thinking, unfair, under all circumstances
I did not give my advice or oppinion. Told me to send my
acct to him, which I had twice sent to Knowles.

Honourable Gentlemen

Presuming on the evident improvement in the
Dock yards (in many respects) and in their agreement with my [inserted above: suggestions]
[text crossed out: reports] and on the judgement of the high abilities that have
referred to [text crossed out: them] [inserted above: my reports] after so long attentively considering them, & Not
forgetting the thanks of the Lords of the Admiralty so handsomely
expressed for [text crossed out: that of] [inserted above: my report of] the 4^th August regarding the Queen Charlotte [inserted above: & by which she was saved]
and the promising hopes of a thorough and comfortable
prevention of [text crossed out: this evil and a great saving in future] [inserted above: the dry rot], I trouble
your Honourable Board with a short memorial laying before
you a [text crossed out: short] statement of part of two years attention [text crossed out: on my]
[text crossed out: part] to a subject of such national consequence that I cannot for
[text crossed out: a] a moment forget it, and the urgency of which has [text crossed out: prevented]
[inserted above: even] interrupted [inserted above: some of] my periodical works to the disappointment of my
friends and consequently my own loss. Confident of your
consideration according to your wonted wisdom and that I shall
be so encouraged for my zeal as that I may persevere in your
[inserted above: laudable] desires [text crossed out: without loss of time] with the best effects I am
Honourable Gentlemen Your very obed.^t Servant
Ja.^s Sowerby

* It was generally supposed that the Dryrot had rendered this ship incurable,
and she is now on service, [text crossed out: bona fide] [inserted above: providentially] saved by my directions; I need
not mention the value of such saving to those conversant with the
subject; it would certainly be enough to afford ample indemnification
for my past services, [text crossed out: but] [inserted above: and] also for future ones, [text crossed out: and] reckoning upon
other first rates [inserted above: &c] assuredly saved and to be saved by such an example
[inserted above: properly applied]
the amount would be immense.


+ On this report I shall be happy to see your desires put in
practice and I have every hope that [inserted above: by] what may now be done
posterity will reap [text crossed out: the] [inserted above: a] benefit, and your Hon: Board [text crossed out: the] [inserted above: their]
blessing.

A short Statement

&c.

1812
July 16 Attended the Honourable Commissioners of H. M. Navy
according to their request transmitted by Mr. Nelson.

17 Reciev'd directions to proceed to Plymouth with Mr.
Lukin, as early as possible, to inspect the H. M. S.
the Queen Charlotte which had recieved considerable
injury from the Dryrot, and to advise the prevention
of its further progress, if possible; ^* [text crossed out: and to extend my]
to report thereon and to extend my report to the
means of prevention on board H. M. S. in future.

21 }
to } Proceeded to Plymouth in company with Mr. Lukin
Aug 1 } attended the Dockyard and H. M. S. the Queen
Charlotte; visited Portsmouth Dockyard in the
way and arrived at Home Aug. 2. being Sunday.

4^th Delivered my report ^* on the Queen Charlotte, pointing
out the possibility of repairing [inserted above: her] and recommending [inserted above: the proper means for]
that purpose [text crossed out: that [inserted above: the removal of] all obstruction to a free current of
air; stretching an awning over the whole ship [text crossed out: at sea] so
placed as to keep off rain and to admit a current of
air beneath it in order to remove all moisture; objecting]

[text crossed out: to the use of artificial heat as likely to encrease the evil,
but recommending ventilation promoted by stoves so placed
as not to heat the Timber under certain restrictions:
recommending the removal of all infected wood, suggesting
the necessity of using the best seasoned Timber in the repairing
and keeping this as dry as possible, putting on the boiled
planks in such a manner that as little moisture as possible
might be confined, & that no part should be painted or
varnished before it was perfectly dry - & Objecting
to chemical impregnations as useless and even detrimental
*]

Aug. 28 [text crossed out: Rec^d the thanks of the Lords of the Admiralty]

1813
Jany 28 Answered application from your Hon: Board
requesting my opinion of Mr. Parkers letter stating
that he had discovered the method of curing the Dryrot

Feby 17 Presented my report on the measures necessary to
be taken for the prevention of the evil of Dryrot on
board H. M. S. in future", [text crossed out: recommending the prop]
[text crossed out: er curing of Timber], [text crossed out: that the Trees be carefully inspected &
if found free from Fungi, should be seasoned as soon as]

[text crossed out: felled, the seasoning to be carried on regularly until the
last moment of fitting together without artificial heat;
that they should be sheltered from rain & the heat of
the sun and supported from the ground; that they should
be kept separate from each other as much as possible
until quite dry; that they should be stacked upon
high ground; recommending the free access of air through
the stacks, not only to prevent fermentation & the
growth of Fungi, but to harden the wood, also that the Ships
should be frequently cleaned [text crossed out: in order to] and all suspicious
parts examined with the Hygrometer in order to discover
the earliest indication of the Dryrot.]

March 24 Further information given as to the address of the makers
of Hygrometer

Oct: 25 Attended Commissioner Seppings to Deptford &
Woolwich Dock yards according to desire of [text crossed out: the]
your Hon: Board expressed on the 16^th

29 Presented my report thereon, pointing out how far
my suggestions relating to stacking of Timber could
be carried into execution &c.

Navy Office 24^th Nov^r 1813.

Sir,

Having attentively considered the two
Reports from you, respecting the mode of
stowing Timber in the Dock Yards, we desire
you will give the necessary instructions to
the Officers of Deptford Dock Yard for the
erection of Six stacks of Timber, to be housed
over according to your plan; - and we
acquaint you that the Officers of that Yard
have had directions to afford you every
assistance in their power in the prosecution
of this business. -

We are,
Sir,
Your humble Servants,
F.J. Hartwell
R. Seppings
H. Legge

James Sowerby Esq^r.

Navy Office 16^th October 1813.

Sir,

With reference to your reports on the
premature decay of His Majesty's Ships,
in which you have made several
observations with respect to the mode
of stowing Timber; We request that you
will attend one of the Surveyors of the
Navy to Deptford and Woolwich Yards
any Morning in the early part of
next week, in order that you may
see the mode of stowing Timber in

how far your suggestions can be
carried into execution. -

James Sowerby Esq^r.
2 Mead Place, Lambeth.

Honourable Gentlemen

According to your desire with reference to my
report of the 17^th ult.^o I can only say at present that Mr Jones
of Oxendon Street, mathematical instrument maker who was a
pupil of Ramsdens, made the instrument I used and at my
desire is about to make some to revolve 10 times, equal to
1000 parts (instead of odd numbers) which will save the trouble
of calculation and agree with the trials by which I calculated
the wetness that is mischievous &c. I waited thus long expecting
to see one every day.

De Luc's instrument is to be had of Mr. Hausmann
of Cumberland Lodge, G^t Windsor Park, with improvements
and the calculations relating to the Thermometer, which when
printed may render his instrument preferable in the opinions
of some persons.

I am

Honourable Gentlemen

Your very obed^t humble Serv^t

Jas Sowerby

2 Mead Place

Lambeth

To the Hon^ble Commissioners of [text crossed out: the] [inserted above: H.M.] Navy

Hon^ble Gentlemen I have rec^d your letter requesting my oppinion on Mr M Barkers [inserted above: stating] of his having discovered the means of preventing the dry rot in Timber you must of course be somewhat aware of the delicacy of my situation, and see that to give an oppinion upon his ideas is as difficult as to interpret Nebuchadnezzers dream, concious of you previous information and judgement I venture to presume that you did not before understand "the dry rot in ships, is unanimously admitted to be caused by an Insect" This and what follows leads me to suppose you are well aware how much this gentleman is likely properly to understand of this serious concern [text crossed out: which has already been so expensively urged] [inserted above: occasionally]

I am Honourable Gentlemen
your obt Hm^ble servant
JS

Navy Office 26^th January 1813.

Sir,

We enclose you the copy of a letter
dated the 7^th instant, from Mr. J. M.
Parker, of the Little Carron Colour Works
at Leith, stating that he has discovered
the means for preventing the Dry Rot
in Timber; and request you to favor
us with your opinion thereon. -

We are,
Sir,
Your humble Servants,
F. J. Hartwell
W. Rule
H Legge

James Sowerby Esq^r
Mead Place
Lambeth.

Copy

Gent^n

The dry rot in Ships, having lately become a
great national loss, besides an incalculable
disappointment, it therefore becomes an object of more
than common importance, to hit upon some mode of
preventing the same - Imagination sometimes leads us
to attempt the performance of impossibilities, and an
attempt to eradicate, or prevent this evil, may be
considered futile or purely imaginable, having hitherto
baffled the most ingenious, yet it may not be
impossible, and I trust my present ideas, will not
appear to be the result of illguided imagination, and
I feel assured that any attempt, likely to attain so
desirable a purpose, will not be discarded by you. -
The dry rot in Ships, is universally admitted to be
caused by an Insect; if so, the remedy will not oppose
such difficulty; but on the contrary, should it be a
natural defect of the wood, or occasioned by the
partial exclusion of atmospheric air, or some other
cause we cannot immediately account for, still, it
does not argue the same cannot be prevented. - I
never have seen a Ship in this situation, but I
have repeatedly seen Oak Timber, in various
situations

The Hon^ble Commissioners
of His Majesty's Navy

situations completely mouldered away, except in some
particular spots, and I presume from the same
cause as in Ships; and in these instances, my
attention has been drawn to the part which
appeared in the decayed mass, perfect, and in
such case, it has been the object of my
pursuit, to find out the cause of such partial
preservation, and by which, I flatter myself
I have found out the means, at a comparative
small expence, to eradically prevent the same. -

Having had the honor to correspond with you on
a former occasion, I thought it proper to make the
present communication to you; feeling assured you
will give the matter that attention, which its importance
deserves. - If it should be found necessary, I could wait
upon you, in order to explain the means I propose,
and on which, I shall be glad of the honor of a reply. -

I am
&c. &c. -
J. M. Parker

Little Carron Colour Works
Leith 7. January 1813. -

The experiments I have made on Wood far
advanced in the dry rot, has been
universally successfull _

If any reference should be necessary, I
beg to mention that I am a relation
of Mr Madgshon, late one of the Masters
of Chatham Yard _

are in consequence to be used generally, we
request that you will state to us the
names and address of the makers of those
Instruments whose construction you prefer.

It is intended to print such parts of
your report as apply to the preservation of
Ships, in order that they may be circulated
among the Officers of the several Yards,
but we request you will inform us whether
you have any further observations to offer
on the subject in order that the Publication

We have now the pleasure to mention
that we are directed by the Lords Commission
ers of the Admiralty to state to you that
they consider you entitled to their thanks

and approbation for your attention and
Report _

We are

Your affectionate Friends.

F. J. Hartwell

H Legge

Navy Office 17. July 1812.

Sir,

We desire you will proceed with Mr. Lionel
Lukin to Plymouth as early as possible, and
inspect the state of His Majesty's Ship Queen
Charlotte, which has received very considerable
injury by the dry rot, with a view to ascertain
what measures it may be advisable to adopt to
stop its further progress if possible, by preventing
the vegetation and spreading of the Fungi.

We further desire that your Report on this
subject may be extended to the measures
necessary to prevent this evil in any of His
Majesty's Ships: and that you will make it
either in conjunction with Mr Lukin or
separately as you may find most convenient.

We are, Sir,
Your humble Servants,
F.J. Hartwell
H Legge
T.B. Thomson

Sowerby Esq^r.
Mead Place
Lambeth.

Private				Navy Office 18^th July 1812

Dear Sir,

I need not repeat to you how much alive
I am to the object of your visit to Plymouth; and I
trust that you will consider the hints that I am
about to give you, as proceeding from an ardent wish
that you may have all possible information on the
subject, and that no circumstance, however minute,
may escape your observation, while you are on the spot.
The fact that you mentioned to Sir William Rule,
of timber that has been heated being more subject
to dry rot than other wood, made me enquire into
the case; and I find that it is proved by practice.
The King's Yacht has a galley between decks, and
the parts where it stands were subject to a great
degree of heat, while attending His Majesty at
Weymouth: Upon the planks being removed, some
time after her return, they, as well as the frame which
was exposed to this heat, were found in a complete

state

state of decay from the growth of Fungi; while the other
parts of the Vessel were free from vegetation ..
It has been the object of Mr. Lukin to
destroy the Boletus by drying up the moisture by
intense heat: for this purpose, stoves were placed
in the Hold and upon the several decks. - The heat
in the Hold must have been very great, as immersion
would prevent the escape of the air; while that on the
decks, I imagine, was not at a very high degree of
temperature. - Is it to the great heat under water
that you attribute the absence of the plant, or to the
action of salt water? It may be right to mention
that the middle and upper decks were particularly
shrunk by heat and of course admitted Atmospheric
air - Can the state of the Thermometer on the several
decks be ascertained? -
It is not only in this ship that Pitch-pine has
proved more subject to dry rot than other wood, but
also in the Bellepoule Frigate .. I saw some specimens
taken from her, which were in the same state as that
which you saw at this Office .. In fact, from all the
observations and enquiries that I have been enabled

to make

to make, timber grown in America is very subject
to decomposition from that cause ..
I should be sorry to misrepresent any circumstance
or take up only one side of the question - Many ships that
have, to the best of my knowledge, been constructed of
English Oak only, have also been destroyed by Boletus
lachrymans. - On this subject you can gain some
local information: the Foudroyant of 80 Guns built
at Plymouth in 1798 was nearly destroyed by it after
three Years' service - the Ajax, 74, built by contract
in 1798, was also found in the same state after the
like service - and many other instances too numerous
to detail here ..
I beg to direct your attention to the small
chocks through which bolts pass to connect the timbers ..
they strike me not only to be injurious to the circulation
of air between the frames, but also, by confining the
carbonic acid gas which issues from the timber (more
particularly where sap is left) to promote the growth
of vegetation .. These chocks are to my mind, not
only useless in themselves, but injurious ..
It will be well to consider whether the ship

is

is sufficiently stripped to ensure her being free from
the Fungi; for a recurrence of the evil, after her
repair, would be extremely unfortunate ..
Some of the Materials (Planks) that have
been taken off are intended to be replaced: it will
be right to ascertain whether they are not so far
infected as to endanger her future safety ..
With every good wish for your pleasant
journey, I remain,

Dear Sir,

Yours very truly
John Knowles

My Dear Sir
I beg to trouble you with this to say that
I have assayed to follow M^r Seppings & your [text crossed out: advice] kind
advice regarding my services to the Navy; for which
accept my most grateful thanks. I can, at present
however, only say, that if I am to reckon my future
services on the basis of what I have done (the common
rate of salvage for my advice on the Queen Charlotte and
something on account of the example by which so many
valuable ships may now be saved, might be [inserted above: deemed] considered
a reasonable indemnification for the loss of time and
the trouble I have had for nearly two years past.) I observe
upon this part of my reports only, the Navy Board would
be indemnified in paying me some compliment if only
as surety for future consideration. While this is neglected
or forgotten I really think important services may be
lost; and this neglect gives me no encouragement
for future exertions, which, by the bye, I grieve to see
necessary, while it must be irksome to a generous mind

to proceed upon such unpromising grounds
I am
Dear Sir
Yours very truly
Ja^s Sowerby

I duly received your note of the 4^th Jan^y observing that no
Timber has been stacked on the Platform, pedestals &c on
account of the brickwork not being sufficiently firm & I
understood from you in conversation that it was to be stacked
in March. I never thought the brickwork would answer the
purpose & Mr Stone had better perhaps not at all depend
upon it but look to some other support.

8^th March 1815.
I am, Sir, your obed^t humble servant
Ja^s Sowerby

Dear Sir

Knowing that any information on the
subject now under your consideration will be acceptable, I
beg to mention, that some of the Timber used in building Ships
of War I believe some part of the planks, are boiled in a
Temperature above 212 of Fah^t. for the purpose of softening
sufficiently to be bent, it may be worth while enquiring if this
may not occasion a partial decomposition of the Fibre of the Wood
or perhaps after being thus softened the Wood may not be
sufficiently dried before being used, you will likely have an
oportunity of observing those planks which have undergone this
operation of boiling, & how far they have suffered in comparison
with others. I am Dear Sir
very truly Yours

the name of the vessel, when it goes,
and to what Wharf, in London. An acre of

peat. The latter any one may take of
the Prince of Wales at 8^d [text crossed out: per] acre

To the Hon: —— Legge

22^d Dec^r 1813.

Dear Sir

Presuming on that friendship which your late
noble Brother was inclined to show me and the kind manner of
your expressing yourself when recollecting me so many years
after I had been a visitor to him at Hayes I take the liberty
of asking your advice as to my concerns with your Honourable
Board, (with which no doubt you are well aquainted) on ac=
=count of the prevalence of the mischief caused by the Dryrot.
As I have now rendered real service in saving His Majesties
Ship the Queen Charlotte from condemnation (which would,
in the commonest case be a sufficient reason for demanding
salvage) and in assisting in various communications on the
subject for the greater part of two years without any fee, reward
or even promise to encourage me; may I request your kind advice
as to my persevering (however unfortunately the dreadful state
of these things require it on the part of the Nation; & however
clearly I may see the means of removing the evil) to the hazard
of sustaining much loss myself by neglecting my own avocations
with no other hope of recompence that at present I have.

I blush at what I have said least it should be premature

but I beg to trust you as a friend.
I am
Dear Sir
Your most faithful
humble Servant

May 27. 1813 Mr Salton called at 5 oClock
to Union place and found the [inserted above: auricularia melina] boletus hybridus &c
had made great ravages in the Kitchen-floor, walls
&c the floor had been new laid about 9 months
since according to the carpenters acct
and was again rotten <u>through</u> in most parts
the place was so situate as to allow of
some certainty of the future prevention
in a moderate degree (I advise) according
was to take some inches of the earth from below
the joists and incline the level to the Area and
again to the cellar perhaps. allowing a grating
for an air course that way only. the joists to
be supported on brick work leaving air room the
skirting to be replaced by stone Parkers cement
to be carried up the walls as a dado and finished
with a [inserted above: small wood] moulding - also in the passage. The

wine Cellar under the stairs and [inserted above: the] support
may have some of the earth which was very
damp cleared away and admittance for air under
the floor of the Kitchen [inserted above: allowed] The Carpenter seems
to comprehend my intentions I therefore trust that
instead of 9 months repairs being wanted it may
without extraordinary casualties which attention
and discernment may much conduce to prevent
being serious — The carpenter would
have advised wainscot rather injudiciously and
also some other matters and he
was also hinted at to replace the [unclear: moss?]
by some friends advice which I
opposed from natural motives &c I wrote this
as a memorandum which may shew by date &c
if occasion requires it, what may be the result.
J. S.

respecting stopping the progress of
that villain the Dry Rot, who has dared

at the bottom of Black Heath Hill.

I remain with Esteem
Your very sincerely
Tho. Telford

My Lord
May I desire your Lordships permission to enquire or
examine into [text crossed out: your Lordships] the dryrot or cause of the disaster
in your Lordships house, mentioned in the papers, as I am
anxious to learn why this evil should so much prevail at a
time when every one seems to possess more information on every
subject than formerly. I might also happen to be somewhat
useful from my experience among the Fungus tribe.
I am My Lord, your Lordships, most humble Servt
Jas. Sowerby
To the Rt. Hon: the Marquis of Lansdowne

Rotting of wood, which developes Fungi, has become
a subject of much importance of late, more parti-
-cularly so, as our Ships have been so dreadfully
affected by it, and as it [inserted above: is] also very dangerous in
private houses. Mr. Sowerby, who lent me these
specimens, from much experience, thinks that the
Dryrot, as it is commonly called, may be best re-
-medied by Judicious management and observation
of Natural means, in place of complicated processes
[text crossed out: and artif] which have generally proved fatal, expensive
and at best hazardous. Mr. S. therefore thinks it
his duty not to withhold his advice if called upon.

The preserving of Fungi is more difficult than the
preservation of wood. Mr. S. has sufficient specimens
of Fungi to show his experience in this, as may
be seen at his museum, where he will be happy to
see any of the present company, on the first or third Tuesdays
in each month, throughout the year.

PHILOSOPHICAL AND CHEMICAL
SOCIETY.

[Line]

REGULATIONS
OF
THE SOCIETY FOR THE INVESTIGATION OF PHILOSOPHICAL
AND CHEMICAL SUBJECTS BY EXPERIMENTS.

[Line]

SECTION I. OF THE CONSTITUTION OF THE SOCIETY.

Art. 1. The Society may consist of two Classes of Members,
resident either in Town or Country.

2. The number of Town Members shall not exceed fifty.

3. The number of Country Members shall be unlimited.

[Left page - Printed text]
2

SECTION II. OF THE VIEWS OF THE SOCIETY.
The object of the Society is absolutely confined to the Investigation by
Experiments of such subjects only as are connected with Che-
mistry, Natural Philosophy, and the Arts and Manufactures
dependent thereon.

SECTION III. OF THE DIRECTION OF THE AFFAIRS OF THE SOCIETY.
Art. 1. The direction of the affairs of the Society shall be vested in a
Committee of Directors, consisting of a President, Treasurer,
and Secretary, and ten Members, of which the President,
Treasurer, Secretary, and one half of the Directors shall
be chosen annually out of all the Town Members, at a General
Meeting, to be holden on the Friday following the first meet-
ing of the Royal Society in each year. The Committee shall
not be competent to transact business, unless three Members,
at least, be present.

2. The Election of the Committee, and all other business brought
forward at a General Meeting of the Society, shall be decided
by ballot: and whenever the votes, on a scrutiny, shall be
found equal, the President shall have an additional vote.

3. All the foregoing Appointments are honorary, and without any
emolument whatever.

4. The Committee shall make such standing Orders and Regula-
tions, for the government of the Society, and the preservation
of order and decorum at its meetings, as they from time to
time shall think proper: subject, however, to the confirmation
or rejection of the next General Meeting of the Society.

It is with great pleasure that I find by your
letter of the 20^th August that my report has met with the
approbation of My Lords Commissioners of the Admiralty
and I feel most highly [text crossed out: honoured] [inserted above: gratified] by their Lordships
thanks [ink blot] I should have done myself the pleasure to
reply sooner, but I wished to make some enquiries
about Hygrometers and although much time has
been spent I hope it has not been entirely lost, as
I have had an opportunity of considering my report,
[inserted above: of collecting my ideas into a regular & concise plan for operation & of]
^ making more numerous observations and further confirming
by them, what appears to me the simplest and most
certain means of accomplishing the end I had in
view. Indeed it was my desire to get an Hygrometer con-
structed of a different form from the one I had used,
and which might be easier applied, but I have found the
workmen so tardy that I have not enough patience to
wait any longer. Should I obtain one that I think
will answer I will submit it to the inspection of your
Honourable Board. The one I used is called Katers Hy-

[inserted above: ([unclear: Dasons] Hygrometer may be used)]
grometer, it was made by Mr. Jones of [unclear: Bronson] Street. It answers
the purpose extremely well, but its divisions require to be
[inserted above: each instrument requires a different calculation]
calculated to bring ^ them to a common standard. Any other
may do, as De Lucs &c where the extremes of wet and dry
are marked. [inserted above: By De Lucs Hy^r] Immersion in water or perfect wetness is
to be considered as 1000^o above perfect dryness. When the index
is below 300^o I find the air is so dry as to be uncomfortable
and injurious to Timber, cracking and opening it as in hot
climates. From 400^o to 650^o in temperate [inserted above: climates] I have found
agreable and safe; above that point the air is sensibly damp
and, more especially if [inserted above: confined] damp and rather warm, supports the
growth of Fungi. From 800^o to 900^o is very dangerous in any
moderate temperature often causing the rot in timber, and
promoting the growth of Fungi: in all cases the danger is aug-
mented by stagnation of the moist air [inserted above: or moisture] and increase
of temperature. I have seen the Lycoperdon carpobolus Linn^s
and S.F. 22 (a little Fungus that discharges a sort of capsule to
a considerable distance like a shell from a mortar)
grow on rotten damp wood both when exposed to frost, and in
a pine heat in the Duke of Portlands [text crossed out: stove] hot house.
There are very few, if any, Fungi that will grow upon
wood which is often covered by water although that wood
may be in a state of rapid decay.
I have before insisted upon the propriety of avoiding

all dark holes or corners where damp may get confined,
or where there is any difficulty in cleaning. I would
in addition recommend, that to discover the earliest
indication of rot the Ship should be often cleaned and
all suspicious parts examined by the Hygrometer. No
place should be allowed to remain long without fresh
air being introduced if the Hygrometer stand above 650^o
if it be above 700^o the air should be often changed or a
current should be passed through or over the place until
the Hygrometer return to the same degree as is indicated by
it in the open air. If this operation be performed in dry
weather it will be most effectual. The instrument should
be allowed to remain a quarter of an hour in the neighbour-
hood of the most suspected wood and the appointed Officers
should pay especial attention to [inserted above: the] middle and lower parts
and [inserted above: dark recesses &] corners of the Ship.
There is no doubt but that [inserted above: Oak &] Mahogany, some kinds of
which I understand are now cheap, and Oak are the best
materials for the large and strong parts of ships where Iron
is not convenient; both may become nearly as hard as Iron
by age and proper attention, as I have myself observed in
Oak, [text crossed out: but] [inserted above: (more especially if Bark left off wood should be examined before use] Deal of any kind is always comparatively soft,
and spongy whatever precaution may be taken, and though
it may be strong enough for some purposes it is very apt

to absorb water, so should be as much avoided as possible, but
most carefully where it is likely to be covered over. Its being
splintery moreover renders it an unmerciful [text crossed out: act] [inserted above: more than can be help^d] to use much
of it in ships of war. I think also that the crowding many
pieces together in place of one larger one is a bad thing. In
the Pitch-pine that was so much decayed on board the Queen
Charlotte, the veins of Turpentine seem to have confined
the moisture and so furthered the decay of the Timber, whereas
in wood of a more uniform texture there is not so great a
stagnation of sap or other fermentable matter and of course
not so congenial a [inserted above: nursery] soil for Fungi.

At page 7 I have said "that I concieve any impregnation is unnecessary
if the wood be properly dried" &c I had considered the endeavour to preserve
Ships by immersing them in Salt water as likely to prove an hindrance
to the more natural method that has most effectually preserved some
Ships formerly for so many years and, as my report says, it is at
best an awkward subterfuge, causing cold damps, detrimental to
those on board and spoiling all metallic substances. I say thus
much at present because I might otherwise appear to have quite
overlooked what I deem to be a most serious evil. Further I
may observe that it has been proposed to pass heated air thro'
tubes, but I presume that there are several reasons that
show this to be dangerous. Artificial heat, I have before shown
is detrimental and this mode of applying it would in the [inserted above: a short]
term of [text crossed out: 100] years, supposing the first principle good, most
likely render it much more so. Most of the modern attempts
to prevent rot, have, from some accidental circumstance ap=
=peared to the ignorant to be successful - the truth of this observa=
=tion is evinced in the case of the Queen Charlotte, for it
never can be supposed that a Stranger as I was to the Board
would have been called in, if the case had not become
desperate after they had tried every means in their [text crossed out: power]
by fire &c and failed; another proof of this is in the case of
charred wood which was for some time thought to be proof
against rot, but as I had [text crossed out: said expected] expressed my opinion, several

instances of rot in charred wood have been shown me and I have
found that charred trees admit certain vegetation on the surface
apparently with as much freedom as other wood.

Thus it will be seen that common natural & simple [text crossed out: means]
principles have the advantage even over erudition, while eru=
=dition commencing with these principles can deeply investigate
the most beautiful subtleties in research and thus find the
surest & completest arguments for [text crossed out: real] true cause & effect.

I would next suggest the necessity

by laying it dry and open, and raised from
the ground near the workmen; and should
any part of the ship have got wet by
any means, it will be proper to let it

and care should be taken that no hollows

sawdust, or any dirt capable of retaining
or absorbing moisture. As it appears

moist, it will be proper to use every

[Margin: As little moisture as possible to be confined.]

[Margin: Parts to be dry when painted or varnished.]
will also be requisite, at this season of

[Margin: Chemical impregnations useless and even detrimental.]
I concieve that any impregnation of
the wood is unnecessary if the wood be
properly dried; and many acid or saline
impregnations I have heard recommended
as perfect cures are certainly detrimental
to the wood, bolts and workmen.

[Margin: Pitch pine to be clear'd, dried & replaced.]
The pitch pine that is to be replaced
should be perfectly cleared of every vestige
of decay and exposed in an open situation
at a distance from the ground and allowed
to be thoroughly dried before it is used
again. I need not insist upon the propriety

of removing all timber that is so rotten
as to be useless.

Desirous of doing the best, I wished to have substituted some one of more general information, and so more capable of the enquiry, but as I knew of nobody who had condescended to study sufficiently the habits of Fungi I had no alternative. Therefore while I might feel proud, in a concern of such magnitude, of the hopes of being able to do good, I could only consider myself as an instrument in the hands of Providence selected for a particular department at a crisis so momentous as the present.

In many cases there is but one way to do right, and this is often hard to find, but sometimes seems so easy that every one is confident of success; [text crossed out: but] [inserted above: however] this confidence is often delusive, as appears in respect to the Dry-rot, the cause of which, confined damp, is so palpable that it might be thought easy to remove^x^, yet from the

[Margin note middle left: ^x^ Methods have often been devised and put into execution which have actually nourished the evil instead of removing it; instances of which are recent and well known.]

[Margin note bottom left: [unclear: Confidence always...]]

day of King Charles^x^ to the present time of increased experience and improvement, its ravages have become more and more distressing.

The confidence I feel of proposing a means of eradicating and preventing this now so peculiarly unfortunate evil arises from an experience that no one else, that I know, has had so long an opportunity of obtaining. The minutiæ of my observations are very tedious and altogether difficult for me to detail, even to persons of much experience, — without some danger of mistakes that might lead to much mischief. I have seen instances of salutary advice misapplied through a wrong or imperfect conception of the principles of it; — I hope therefore to have time [inserted above: allowed] for a fuller report, but since in the immediate object of my enquiry, the probability of repairing His Majesty's Ship Queen Charlotte, which appears to me capable of being repaired, the delay of a day is truly alarming, I shall now suggest those

I first saw his Majesty's Ship Queen Charlotte on the 24^th of July & again on the 25^th I found that great quantities of Timber had been removed from her & that it was intended still to take out more which appeared to me to be proper: the lower decks and hold were very close, requiring air; there were more or less ves=tiges of Fungi from the middle deck downwards; they were of the following species; _Boletus hybridus_ E: F: tab: 289 being the young state of _Boletus medulla=panis_ E: F: 326 (White rugged soft Fungi;) _Xylostroma giganteum_ (A gigantic Leather-like Fungus peculiar to rotten Oak) E: F: 358. _Auricularia pulverulenta_ E: F: 214. and some others of but little con=sequence. The various habits of these different species would, if attended to, point out the proper cautions necessary to prevent their growth. There were very little [inserted above: most of] remains of _Boletus lachrymans_ E: F: 113, the best

To the Honourable Commissioners of H.M. Navy.

Honourable Gentlemen

It is with great pleasure that I find by
your letter of the 28^th of August that my
report has met with the approbation of
My Lords Commissioners of the Admiralty.
I feel most highly gratified by their Lord=
=ships thanks.

I [text crossed out: should] [inserted above: ought] not [inserted above: to] have let so much time
elapse, but have done myself the pleasure
to reply sooner; the delay is however
fortunate, for whatever confidence I
had of success, arising from a continual
watchfulness over the habits of Fungi
for many years, yet to others I could
not but consider it as a new subject

and very convenient to have some sort
of evidence of probable success for
future encouragement, which from
previous experience I felt the more
desirous of, as the concern is of so much
magnitude that more hasty conclusions
might have been liable to censure
for want of that proper and favourable
evidence that may now, I hope, be fairly
admitted; besides I have had an opportunity
of considering my report, of collecting
my ideas into a regular and concise
plan for operation, and of making
more numerous observations and further
confirming by them, what appears to
me the simplest and most certain means
of accomplishing so desirable an end.

As their Lordships have been pleased
to direct that the means I have had the
honour to recommend to check the ra=
=vages of the Dry rot on board H.M.
S. the Queen Charlotte should be
carried strictly into execution*, I

Navy Office 17^th July 1812

Sir

We desire you will proceed with Mr Lionel Lukin to Plymouth, as early as possible, & inspect the state of His Majesty's Ship Queen Charlotte, which has recieved very considerable injury by the Dry rot, with a view to ascertain what measures it may be advisable to adopt to stop its further progress if possible, by preventing the vegetation & spreading of the Fungi.

We further desire that your report on this subject may be extended to the measures necessary to prevent this evil in any of His Majesty's Ships; and that you will make it either in conjunction with Mr Lukin or separately, as you may find most convenient.

We are, Sir

Your humble Servants

F. T. Hartwell

H. Legge

T. B. Thompson.

— Sowerby Esq^r

Mead Place

Lambeth.

To the Honourable Commissioners of His Majesty's Navy.

Honourable Gentlemen

Shocked at the report of the rotting state of many of our National Bulwarks I felt ready to make enquiry as to the probability of curing or preventing the evil; when, in consequence of my known experience among subjects which thrive under the same circumstances as those which produce and develope the decay of Timber, viz. Fungi, I happened to be called upon for that purpose; & I therefore felt it an imperative duty, notwithstanding my other necessary occupations to attend immediately to the subject, & not less so when I perceived the penetration and anxiety of your Honourable Board by the questions you asked me; my answers to which, when in any degree to the purpose were treasured up in a manner that convinced me how very desirous you were to execute with fidelity your most arduous trust.

Desirous of doing the best I wished to have substituted some one of more general information & so more capable of the enquiry, but as I know of nobody who had condescended to study sufficiently the habits of Fungi, I had no alternative. Therefore, while I might feel proud, in a concern of such magnitude of the hopes of being able to do good, I could only consider myself as an instrument in the hands of Providence selected for a particular department at a crisis so momentous as the present.

In many cases there is but one way to do right & this is often hard to find, but sometimes seems so easy that every one is confident of success; this confidence, however, is often delusive as appears in respect to the Dry-rot, the cause of which,

[margin note: [unclear: and damp / cause of the / Dry rot.]]

Confined Damp, is so palpable that it might be thought easy to
remove;* yet, from the day of King Charles^† to the present time
of increased experience & improvement, its ravages have become
more and more distressing.

The confidence I feel of proposing a means of
[Margin note: perfectly know / means of sufficient / to execute / the means / putting them]
eradicating & preventing this now so peculiarly unfortunate evil
arises from an experience that no one else that I know, has had
so long an opportunity of obtaining. The minutiae of my observations
are very tedious and altogether difficult for me to detail, even to
persons of much experience, without some danger of mistakes that
might lead to much mischief. I have seen instances of salutary
[Margin note: salutary advice / misapplied]
advice misapplied through a wrong or [text crossed out: perfect] imperfect conception
of the principles of it; I hope, therefore, to have time allowed
for a fuller report, but since [inserted above: in] the immediate object of my enquiry,
the probability of repairing His Majesty's Ship Queen Charlotte
[Margin note: the Queen Charlotte / capable of being / repaired.]
which appears to me capable of being repaired, the delay of a
day is truly alarming, I shall now suggest those means Nature
has pointed out to me as promising the greatest security, happily
at but little or no additional expence.

I first saw His Majesty's Ship the Queen Charlotte
on the 24^th of July & again on the 25^th I found that great
quantities of Timber had been removed from her & that it was

* Methods have often been devised & put into execution which have actually
nourished the evil instead of removing it; instances of which are recent
& well known.

† The Foudroyant of 80 Guns built at Plymouth in 1798 & the Ajax, built
by contract in 1798 were nearly destroyed by Fungi in the space of about
three years.

intended still to take out more, which appeared to be proper;
the lower decks & hold were very close, requiring air; there were
more or less vestiges of Fungi from the middle deck downwards;
[Margin note: on board / Queen Charlotte]
they were of the following species, Boletus hybridus E.F. tab.
289, being the young state of Boletus medulla-panis E.F. 326.
(White rugged soft Fungi) Xylostroma giganteum (A Gigantic
Leather-like Fungus peculiar to rotten Oak) E.F. 358. Auricu-
laria pulverulenta E.F. 214 & some others of but little con-
[Margin note: knowledge of / habits of Fungi / necessary for the / cure of the Dry-rot]
sequence. The various habits of these different species would,
if attended to, point out the proper cautions necessary to pre-
vent their growth. There were [text crossed out: but] [inserted above: very] little remains of Boletus
Lachrymans E.F. 113, the best known Dryrot. On the
morning of the 25^th an Hygrometer was left for an hour near
the pump well in the Hold; it indicated a very damp air
standing at 828·6° outside the ship it had stood at 464·2°
perfect wetness being indicated by 1000°. I should recommend
[Margin note: the use of the / Hygrometer / recommended.]
the general use of this instrument as likely to point out the
state of air favourable for the growth of Fungi. I have found
by experiment that Wood will rot & Fungi grow at 800° or 900°.

I have ventured to declare that the Queen Charlotte
may be repaired, because I have hopes that the further pro-
gress of vegetation may be stopped, but unusual caution is
requisite with her from the circumstance of the Timber of
which she is built being in a state fit for vegetation when-
ever it is placed in a favourable situation.

In the first place I should recommend the removal
[Margin note: air without / rain to be admitted.]
of every obstruction to a free current of air through and around
every part possible and stretching an awning over the whole
ship at some distance above the deck and so placed as to keep

off rain without dripping upon the vessel & at the same time to
admit a current of air beneath it; the awnings at present in
use are not sufficiently extensive, they are pervious to rain &
lie so close to the deck that there is some reason to fear that by
confining moisture they may promote destructive fermentation,
the immediate cause of the rot, which is the more probable in the
present favourable season for the growth of Fungi. Thus, by a
[Margin note: moisture / removed by / current of air]
current of air I would endeavour to remove all moisture &
so leave no nourishment for the parasite; I would even sug-
[Margin note: a person should / be appointed to / direct these / operations]
gest the appointment of some able person to see the various
parts as thoroughly dried as possible, by air, before any
[Margin note: artificial heat / objectionable.]
fresh wood is put in. I should object to the use of artificial
heat as likely to increase the temperature of the moisture &
consequently the fermentation without removing it; but a rapid
[Margin note: ventilation / promoted by / stoves may be / useful under certain / restrictions.]
draught of cold air which may be caused by stoves so placed
as not to heat the Timber, will be useful to dry such parts
as the air can otherwise scarcely have access to, this kind of
ventilation Mr. Lukin has already employed with success.

I would next suggest the necessity of using the best
[Margin note: best seasoned / timber to be used / & kept dry.]
seasoned Timber in the repair and to keep this as dry as possible
by laying it dry and open and raised from the ground near
the workmen and should any part of the Ship have got wet
[Margin note: no fresh wood / should be placed / over wet parts.]
by any means it will be proper to let it dry before fresh wood
is placed over it; and care should be taken that no hollows
are covered up that contain shavings, sawdust or any dirt capable
[inserted above: or absorbing]
of retaining moisture. As it appears necessary to put the boiled
[Margin note: boiled planks / to be put on / alternately / to dry.]
planks on while moist it will be proper to use every endeavour
to let the planks have air enough to dry internally, thus they may
be put on alternately, leaving spaces to be filled by intermediate

[Margin: moisture is to be avoided] ones after the first set have had sufficient time to dry; two or three
planks may be kept off 'till all the others are supposed to be thoroughly
dry that when the whole is closed, as little moisture as possible may
be confined. It will also be requisite, at this season of the year,
[Margin: to be dry before painted or washed.] particularly, not to paint any part that is not previously as dry
as possible, so that the oil may rather penetrate the wood itself
than drive moisture before it.
[Margin: chemical impregnations useless if not detrimental.] I concieve that any impregnation of the wood is
unnecessary, if the wood be properly dried; and many acid [inserted above: and or]
saline impregnations I have heard recommended as perfect cures,
are certainly detrimental to the wood, bolts & workmen.
[Margin: Pitch Pine to be clean & dry before replaced.] The Pitch Pine that is to be replaced should be perfectly
cleared of every vestige of decay and exposed in an open situation
at a distance from the ground & allowed to be thoroughly dried be-
fore it is used again. I need not insist upon the propriety of re-
moving all Timber that is so rotten as to be useless.
These, seemingly trifling preliminaries, if not
understood clearly, may be carried with honest ardour to such
extremes as to contradict one another, or through inattention to
[Margin: inattention to minutiæ productive of danger] minutiæ may be so executed as not to answer the intended purpose,
and I feel so highly concerned that I dread any misapplication
As the assiduous, but misguided endeavours of some to do good
will establish the foundation of this fear, I shall take the
liberty to repeat an instance I observed on board the Queen Char-
lotte, and add a circumstance or two which have occurred on board
another of His Majesty's Ships: 1^st in endeavouring to keep off
[Margin: coverings now on Decks of the Queen Charlotte ruinous.] rain coverings have been laid so close to the Deck as to encourage
vegetation by enclosing wet that had accidentally got through
some holes, in the same way as, if on the ground floor of my

house on a level with Land springs I leave a Board or piece of Floor
Cloth for a few days, the Dryrot is sure to grow under it. On board
[Margin: rubbish enclosed in hollows of the San Domingo productive of Dryrot.] another Ship, the San Domingo, with the laudable design of preventing
the fall of chips, rain, rubbish &c into several hollows, these hollows
have been covered over without clearing away what had already got
in, thus securing the damp stagnated air with the mischief it was
nursing, which, if increased, will of course have its concomitant
evils & besides various species of Fungi, their attendant insects, as
[Margin: abundance of Insects] appears particularly on board this Ship, where the Oniscus asellus
of Linnæus or Common Hoglouse has become so abundant as
to remind us of the plagues of Egypt & spread abroad so much
as even to appear at the Officers meals. On board this same
[Margin: want of cleanliness & removal of rubbish not enough attended to in the obscure parts of Ships.] Ship much proper attention has been paid to the admission
of air by [text crossed out: means] [inserted above: the use] of Lattice work, yet I noticed with regret
that many of the obscure parts of the vessel were defective in
that kind of cleanliness necessary to keep her moderately dry
and disturb the haunts of Insects.
I have been extremely anxious to make this report
as soon as possible; I could have made several observations relating
to the mode of stacking the Timber intended for immediate use,
of preserving it after being wrought &c, but I was unwilling to
enter into the latter part of my examination without more infor-
mation, fearing I might say too little or too much to be intelligible.
The haste with which I have drawn up these obser-
vations relating to the Queen Charlotte, added to the circumstance
of its being the first time of my making an Official Report, I
beg to plead as an excuse for such inaccuracies or omissions as
may occur.
I did not join Mr. Lukin in a report, as I had

reason to suppose that what he would have said would only have
been a repetition of former reports, of which he told me he had
given many; and I was desirous of giving my individual
opinion, that, should it be found consistent with other reports
it might be acted upon with greater confidence, but if it differed,
the experience of the Board would enable them to judge impartially.
I must now express my gratitude to Mr. Lukin
for the assistance he has given me & his assiduity in pointing
out what most nearly related to the subject. Among other things
the circumstance of the hollows containing rubbish being covered
[Margin: x on board the San Domingo.] up^x, is one, for the knowledge of which I am particularly indebted
to him.
I am
Honourable Gentlemen
Your very obed^t Servant
Ja^s Sowerby
4^th August 1812.

Navy Office 28^th August 1812

Mr. Sowerby

Having furnished the Lords Commissioners
of the Admiralty with a Copy of your report dated the 14^th
instant relative to the measures necessary to be adopted to prevent
the progress of the Dryrot in His Majesty's Ship Queen Charlotte,
their Lordships have been pleased to direct that the means which
you have recommended, shall be carried into execution in repairing
that Ship as well as in building & repairing His Majesty's
Ships at the several Yards; And as Hygrometers are in conse-
-quence to be used generally, we request that you will state to us
the names and address of the makers of those instruments whose
construction you prefer.

It is intended to print such parts of your report
as apply to the preservation of Ships, in order that they may
be circulated among the Officers of the several Yards, but we
request you will inform us whether you have any further ob-
-servations to offer on the subject, in order that the publication
may be complete.

We have now the pleasure to mention that we
are directed by the Lords Commissioners of the Admiralty
to state to you that they consider you entitled to their thanks
and approbation for your attention and Report.

We are

Your affectionate Friends.

F. J. Hartwell

H: Legge

T. B. Thompson

Copy)

"Gent.^n The Dryrot in [text crossed out: Timber] Ships having lately become
a great national loss, besides an incalculable disappointment, it
therefore becomes an object of more than common importance, to
hit upon some mode of preventing the same; Imagination some-
-times leads us to attempt the performance of impossibilities; and
an attempt to eradicate or prevent this evil may be considered
futile or purely imaginable, having hitherto baffled the most inge-
-nious, yet it may not be impossible, and I trust my present
ideas will not appear to be the result of illguided imagination
and I feel assured that any attempt, likely to attain so desirable
a purpose will not be discarded by you. -

The Dryrot in Ships is universally admitted to be
caused by [inserted above: an] Insect; if so, the remedy will not oppose much diffi-

-culty; but on the contrary, should it be a natural defect of the wood
or occasioned by the partial exclusion of atmospheric air, or some other
cause we cannot immediately account for, still it does not
argue the same cannot be prevented. I never have seen
a Ship in this situation, but I have repeatedly seen Oak
Timber in various situations completely mouldered away,
except in some particular spots, and I presume from the
same cause as in Ships; and in these instances my attention
has been drawn to the part which appeared in the decayed
mass perfect; and in such case it has been the object of my
pursuit to find out the cause of such partial preservation, &
by which, I flatter myself, I have found out the means
at a comparative small expence, to radically prevent the
same.

Having had the honour to correspond with you
on a former occasion, I thought it proper to make the present
communication to you, feeling assured you will give the matter
that attention which its importance deserves. If it should be
found necessary I could wait upon you in order to [text crossed out: give you]
explain the means I propose, and on which I shall be glad
of the honour of a reply.

I am &c &c

I. M. Parker.

The Hon^ble Commissioners of
His Majesty's Navy.

Little Carron Colour Works
Leith 7^th Jan^y 1813

The Experiments I have made on wood far advanced in the Dryrot
has been universally successful.

If any reference should be necessary, I beg to mention that I am
a relation of Mr. Madgshon late one of the Masters of Chatham Yard

To the Honourable Commissioners of H. M. Navy.

Honourable Gentlemen
I have received your letter requesting my opinions
on Mr. J. M. Packers stating his having discovered the means of
preventing the [text crossed out: means of pre] dryrot in Timber. You, of course,
must be somewhat aware of the delicacy of my situation & see
that to give an opinion upon his ideas is as difficult as to interpret
Nebuchadnezzars dream. Conscious of your previous information
and judgement I venture to presume that you did not before
understand the Dryrot in Ships as unanimously admitted to be
caused by an Insect. This, and what follows leads me to suppose
you are well aware how much this Gentleman is likely properly
to understand of this serious concern.
I am
Honourable Gentlemen
Your Obedient humble Servant
Jas: Sowerby.
28 January 1813

To the Honourable Commissioners of His Majesty's Navy.

Honourable Gentlemen.
It is with great pleasure that I find by your
letter of the 28^th August that my report has met with the appro-
bation of My Lords Commissioners of the Admiralty, I feel most
highly gratified by their Lordships thanks.
I ought not to have let so much time elapse
but have done myself the pleasure to reply sooner; the delay, is,
however, fortunate, for whatever confidence I had of success,
arising from a continual watchfulness over the habits of Fungi
for many years, yet to others I could not but consider it as
[inserted above: a] new subject and very convenient to have some sort of evidence
of probable success for future encouragement, which, from pre-
vious experience I felt the more desirous of, as the concern is
of so much magnitude that more hasty conclusions might
have been liable to censure for want of that proper and
favourable evidence that may now, I hope, be fairly admitted;
besides, I have had an opportunity of considering my report,
of collecting my Ideas into a regular & concise plan for operation
and of making more numerous observations & further con-
firming by them what appears to me the simplest & most cer-
tain means of accomplishing so desirable an end.
As their Lordships have been pleased to direct
that the means I have had the honour to recommend to check
the ravages of the Dry rot on board His Majesty's Ship the
Queen Charlotte should be carried strictly into execution * I

* I cannot but express that I am deeply sensible of the honour their
Lordships have done me in sending S^r W^m Rule & M^r Knowles

sincerely hope they will prove successful, but it must be expected
that a ship which has suffered so much can never be rendered
quite so sound as a ship built at first with proper materials &
due precaution

[Margin note: not aware anything material is omitted in report relating to Queen Charlotte other Ships may be finished upon the same principles] I am not aware that I have omitted any material
circumstance relating to that Ship and I believe that by applying
the same principles in finishing anyother Ship as your Honourable
Board have been pleased to suggest, much may be done towards
the total prevention of the rot. Should the operations I have
recommended for the Queen Charlotte continue to be attended
with the success I anticipated, they will give confidence, exciting
more particular attention & becoming a powerful stimulus to
further exertion.

In the mean time, as your Honourable Board
"further require that my report may be extended to the measures
necessary to prevent the evil in any of His Majesty's Ships"
I beg leave to offer the following observations relating to the
seasoning of Timber, a subject, upon the discussion of which
[Margin note: for the timber of future Ships to be carefully felled & if found free from fungi to be sea-soned as soon as without heat] for want of time I had not entered in my former report.

The trees, having been felled at a proper
time of year and upon examination found to be free from internal
Fungi & rot * the seasoning should be immediately commenced
and carried on regularly until the last moment of fitting
[Margin note: timber to be kept under shelter and off the ground while seasoning] together, without artificial heat; they should even be sheltered
from rain & the heat of the sun & supported from the ground
upon which they should never afterw be suffered to lie quiet long;

with my report to Plymouth, since in their experienced hands I have
the satisfaction to learn it has been successful in a case rendered
more desperate by the mistakes that had preceded my report.

* An externally good looking living Oak has been so full of the rot as to

[Margin note: kept distant / from each other] they should be kept separate from one another at all times, especially

when not quite dry and if brought from distant Countries by sea,

[Margin note: put dry on / board & / stowed so as / to prevent.] they should be put on Shipboard as dry as possible & not stowed

too close, for incipient fermentation is much to be feared, espe=

=cially if the Timber be fresh cut; it destroys the connection of the

fibres and is difficult to check.

[Margin note: Stacks of Timber / very open & / on high ground.] The Timbers having been squared & reduced to a

certain state of dryness should be stacked upon high ground, the

lowest ones being raised some feet from the [text crossed out: groun] earth; the next

supported at a proper distance, perhaps twice the measure of its

thickness and those nearest to the top of the stack may be closest.

The distance of the Timbers from each other may vary with the situa=

=tion of the Stacks and the state of the wood; those most confined, in

the lowest ground or containing freshest wood being most open,

[Margin note: Free access of air / necessary.] since the free access of proper air not only prevents fermentation

and the growth of Fungi, but tends to harden the wood & so renders

it less liable to be injured by damp hereafter and more unfit for

the nourishment of the Dryrot.

[Margin note: High stacks / preferable to long / low ones.] High stacks are better than long ranges,

particularly in low confined marshy Countries, or adjacent

[Margin note: Stacks to be so / arranged as not to / prevent the air / from pervading the / openings.] to our coast; and one stack should never be placed so as

to prevent the wind from entering the open spaces of

another.

[Margin note: If Fungi appear / Timber must be / cleaned & fresh / stacked.] Should the wood show any signs of

Fungi, which, notwithstanding much caution, may sometimes

happen when the Timber has been placed too close, stacked

too early, or from a succession of close, damp weather,

it must be cleaned & occasionally turned & fresh stacked.

[text crossed out: lest it be easily crumbled to pieces:] it is proper to observe if there be any

infant signs of this before using it.

[Margin note: Stacks may / be taken down & / rebuilt nearer / the hands of the / builder.] It will be found generally useful to pull down

the stacks after a short time and build them afresh nearer * the

place where the Timber is to be used, the upper Timbers being

brought to the bottom of the fresh stack, the top of which will

be formed by those that were at the bottom of the old stack; this

movement being repeated several times will facilitate & promote

the healthy seasoning of the wood & at the same time bring it

nearer to the hands of the builder * In this way of proceeding

the time required to remove Timber from the place where it was

felled may be so economized as to advance the seasoning very

[Margin note: By this mode of / proceeding much / time may be / gained.] considerably; constantly recollecting to keep it from rain & the

wet ground; thus in many cases much time may be gained. If

Timber cut at different periods be placed in the same stack it

will not be difficult to regulate the turning of the stacks so as

to bring that which is most seasoned to the workmens hands

[Margin note: Timber should be / worked & the ship / built under cover / with a free passage / for air, the seasoning / going on still all / the time.] first. The wood that is removed for the use of the builders

should, all the time it is in their hands, be kept under shelter

from rain even when worked into the frame ^ [inserted above: which should be placed under covering], and as high as

possible in the stocks with a free passage for air around the

very keel.

These proceedings being attended to, the Timber

may be considered as seasoning more & more until the Ship

[Margin note: Stacks may be / arranged orna= / mentally.] * It might be of advantage if some smart arrangement could be

made for order & convenience in placing the wood according

to its most appropriate purposes, ornamentally imitative of the

Muskets in an armoury. It would become the boast of some in=

=dividuals, whose skill is naturally adapted to it, and at length

the pride of the nation to contemplate on a neat & cleanly ar=

=rangement, pleasing in the present & offering a fair prospect for

the future.

is finished, when she need not remain in Dock or Ordinary for

seasoning and if then kept clean & dry according to observations

in a former report she may still be improving the quality of her

Timbers, getting the better for wear, and absolutely increasing in value

for a long time, even so as at the end of another century to vie

with the Royal William instead of requiring to be almost rebuilt

in a few years. By this mode of seasoning there will always be

wood free from rot or Fungi & fit for building durable Ships;

the Timber yards also, being kept clean, all the repositories will

have a more comfortable pleasant & wholesome appearance.

I do not doubt, after the late prevailing unfortunate

circumstances that the wisdom of your Honourable Board will

contrive that every requisite attention shall be paid to the subject &

will also determine upon the means to carry so great a concern into

execution in the most proper manner.

As "Hygrometers are to be used generally and

as I am requested to state the names & addresses of the makers

of the instruments whose construction I prefer," I beg leave to

state that it was my desire to get an Hygrometer constructed of

a different form from the one I had used and which might be

easier applied, but I have not yet got it completed. Should I

obtain one that I think will answer I will submit it to the

inspection of your Honourable Board. The one I used is called

[Margin note: Kater's Hygrometer / the one used.] Katers Hygrometer, it was made by Mr Jones of Oxendon Street

[Margin note: Its divisions require / calculation.] It answers the purpose extremely well, but its divisions require

to be calculated to bring them to a common standard, & each

[Margin note: Various others will / do.] instrument requires a different calculation. Any other in

which the extremes of wet and dry are marked, may do, as De

Luc's &c. Immersion in water or perfect wetness is considered

as 1000^o above perfect dryness.

[Marginal note: Below 300^o injurious] When the Index is below 300^o the air is so dry
as to be uncomfortable & injurious to Timber, cracking & opening it
[Marginal note: From 400^o to 600^o safe] as in hot climates. From 400^o to 600^o in our vicinity I have found
agreeable & safe; above that point the air is sensibly damp &
more especially if confined and rather warm, supports the
[Marginal note: From 800^o to 900^o dangerous] growth of Fungi: From 800^o to 900^o is very dangerous in any moderate
temperature, often causing the rot in Timber & promoting the growth
[Marginal note: Danger augmented by stagnation & heat] of Fungi, in all cases the danger is augmented by stagnation of
the moist air or moisture & increase of temperature. I have seen
[Marginal note: Some Fungi at any temperature others require a pine heat & moist] the Lycoperdon carpobolus (of Linneus & E. Fungi tab. 22. a little Fungus
that discharges a sort of capsule to a considerable distance like a shell
from a mortar) grow on rotten damp wood both when exposed
to frost out of doors and in a pine heat in the late Duke of
Portlands hot house at Bulstrode. There are few Fungi that
[Marginal note: Wood may rot without indication of Fungi.] will grow upon wood that is covered by water although that
wood may be in a state of rapid decay.

I have before insisted upon the propriety
of avoiding all dark holes or corners where damp may get
confined or where there is any difficulty in cleaning; I would
[Marginal note: When cleaning an examination with Hygrometer is recommended to discover the first indication of rot.] in addition recommend that to discover the earliest indications
of the rot the Ship should be often cleaned & all suspicious
parts examined with the Hygrometer. No place should be
allowed to remain long without fresh air being introduced
[Marginal note: If the Hygrometer is above 700^o the air must be often changed.] if the Hygrometer stands above 600^o, if it be above 700^o the air
should be often changed or a current should be passed through
or over the place until the Hygrometer return to the same degree
as is indicated by it in the open air. If this operation be per-
[Marginal note: The best time] formed in dry weather it will be most effectual. The instrument

should be allowed to remain a quarter of an hour in the neigh-
bourhood of the most suspected wood^* and the appointed Officers
should pay especial attention to the middle & lower parts and
dark recesses or corners of Ships

There is no doubt but that Oak and Mahogany,
some [text crossed out: parts] [inserted above: kinds] of which I understand are now cheap, are good ma-
terials for the large & strong parts of Ships where Iron is not
convenient; Oak, more especially English, may become nearly
[Marginal note: Deal a bad wood for Ships.] as hard as Iron by age and proper attention. Deal of any kind
is always comparatively soft and spongy, whatever precaution
may be taken, and though it may be strong enough for some purposes,
it is very apt to absorb water so should be as much avoided as possible
but most carefully where it is likely to be covered over. Its being
[Marginal note: Uniting several pieces in place of one larger one bad.] splintery renders it an unmerciful act to use more than can be
helped in Ships of war. I think also that the crowding many
pieces together in place of one larger one is a bad thing.

Every Englishman must feel grateful to your
Honourable Board for the pains you have taken in your arduous
trust. With the best hope that your endeavours may be crowned
with the most brilliant success I remain
Honourable Gentlemen
Your very faithful & humble Servant
Jas. Sowerby

2 Mead Place Lambeth 17^th Feby 1813.

^* It would be proper also to make allowance for the temperature in determining
the degrees of moisture (for the higher the temperature the lower the dangerous
point [inserted above: on the instrument]) for which tables may be constructed as soon as
the kind of Hygrometer best adapted to the purpose is determined on.
